teacher_guide.big_idea: Career clusters help students organize many different jobs into meaningful groups based on common interests, skills, and workplace contexts.
learning_goals: I can explain what a career cluster is.
I can sort jobs and short profiles into appropriate career clusters using evidence.
I can defend which career cluster feels like my best current fit and why.
materials: job and profile examples on slides

timing.activity: Open with a quick cluster challenge and pair talk about how jobs can be grouped.
Teach the idea of career clusters and model how to sort using evidence.
Guide students through a case-card style sort with short profiles and job examples.
Students complete an interactive worksheet with justifications and personal fit reflection.
Students complete a defend-your-choice exit ticket and quick share-out.

teacher_notes: Keep the tone exploratory rather than pressuring students to choose one career now.
Encourage evidence-based sorting rather than guessing from stereotypes or status.
Use discussion moves that make students justify why a cluster fits, not just name one quickly.
Keep teacher-only prompts and extension notes out of student-facing materials.

content.subtitle: How can career clusters help us explore possible future pathways?

content.scenario: Three jobs appear on the screen: paramedic, video game designer, and welder. If you had to sort them quickly into career clusters, what evidence would help you decide?
prompts: What clues help you sort each job?
Which job feels easiest to place?
Which job might lead to debate, and why?

rows: Look at what the person mainly does, not just the job title.
Think about the skills, tools, and work setting involved.
Choose the best fit and be ready to defend your reasoning.

content.left: Profile: enjoys helping people in stressful situations, learning about the body, and working in fast-paced teams.
content.right: Cluster choices: health science | human services
prompts: Which cluster is the best fit right now?
What evidence makes that cluster stronger than the other one?

content.task: Use the worksheet to sort each job or short profile into the best-fitting career cluster and defend your decisions.
prompts: Student profile: loves building, measuring, and using tools
Student profile: enjoys coding, troubleshooting, and designing digital products
Student profile: likes helping children learn and grow
Which cluster feels like your best current fit?
What strengths or interests connect you to that cluster?
What makes that cluster a better fit than one other option?

anchor: A career cluster is a group of related jobs and pathways.
Strong sorting uses evidence about the work, skills, and setting.
The goal is best fit, not perfect certainty.
worksheet.tip: When two clusters seem possible, choose the stronger fit and explain why it fits better.

questions.q_text: Complete a quick sort for these jobs: paramedic, chef, welder, software developer, educational assistant, graphic designer. For each one, name the best-fitting career cluster and one clue that helped you decide.
Choose two jobs that could fit more than one cluster. Defend which cluster is the stronger fit for each one.

Read these two short profiles and decide which cluster fits each one best: (a) likes coding, logic, and solving digital problems; (b) likes coaching, helping, and working with children. Explain your reasoning.
Which career cluster feels like your best current fit, and which one feels like a possible stretch option? Explain your thinking using interests, strengths, or curiosities.

self_check: Did I sort using evidence instead of guessing?
Did I defend at least one tricky cluster choice?
Did I connect one cluster to my own interests or strengths?
answer_key: Sample clusters: paramedic -> health science; chef -> hospitality and tourism; welder -> skilled trades; software developer -> information technology; educational assistant -> education and training; graphic designer -> arts, A/V technology, and communications.

exit_ticket.prompt: Choose one career cluster that feels like your best current fit and one other cluster that could also fit you. Explain why your best-fit cluster is stronger right now, using your interests, strengths, or curiosities as evidence.
success_criteria: I named one best-fit cluster clearly.
I compared it to one other possible cluster.
I used personal evidence from my interests, strengths, or curiosities.
I used the exit ticket as my final evidence location.
answer_key: Answers will vary. A strong answer names a best-fit cluster, compares it to another option, and justifies the choice with personal evidence.
